game_name: Utopia: The Creation of a Nation

design_philosophy.core_loop: Build and manage a civilization from founding through development, balancing resources, population, and infrastructure
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Managing limited resources and competing development priorities while defending against external threats
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Nation builder - creating a prosperous society from scratch and guiding it toward utopian ideals
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Strategy game that emphasizes simulation depth and systemic complexity over narrative, placing player agency in economic and administrative systems rather than scripted progression

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player can adjust simulation parameters and difficulty settings before starting; game difficulty emerges from systemic interactions rather than preset challenges

progression.upgrade_system: Technology and infrastructure unlocks through research and construction; civilization advances through cumulative building and development choices
progression.unlock_mechanism: New structures, technologies, and capabilities unlock as population grows, resources accumulate, and prerequisites are met
replayability_hooks: Multiple starting scenarios and map configurations
Different civilization types with distinct strengths
Emergent gameplay from systemic interactions produces varied outcomes
Sandbox-style building with no single win condition

world_building_techniques: Player constructs world through city-building and territorial expansion
Civilization development serves as implicit narrative arc
Environmental features and resources shape strategic narrative

faction_indicators: Different civilization types embody distinct societal philosophies
Player choices in development priorities reflect values (militarism vs. culture vs. commerce)
Population happiness and social stability indicate governance philosophy
danger_signals: Resource depletion and famine mechanics create existential pressure
Military threats from rival civilizations introduce conflict
Overpopulation and infrastructure strain create societal stress
environmental_storytelling: Map terrain and resources constrain and inspire strategic direction
Built structures accumulate to form distinctive civilizations
Territory control and borders define player's domain and influence

data_quality_note: Source provides only brief mention confirming game existence (1991 computer strategy game). Detailed design philosophy, difficulty, and progression data inferred from paradigm classification as sim-strategy and genre conventions. Heavy reliance on T3 qualitative assessment.
